facts_disclosed: Considering part-time Lofoten field station position

turns.content: I have been asked to weigh in on something that is not strictly a scientific question, though it has scientific dimensions. There is a part-time position opening at a field station in Lofoten. I have been approached about it. The station focuses on nearshore habitat monitoring, kelp beds in particular, and they need someone who can run the aquaculture research programme two weeks per month. The rest of my time would remain in Tromsoe.

That sounds like a significant opportunity. A split appointment between Tromsoe and Lofoten could give you direct access to field sites that would be hard to replicate from a single location. What does the kelp aquaculture programme at the Lofoten station look like currently -- is it established, or would you be building it from the ground up?

Somewhere in between. They have infrastructure -- mooring points, a small wet lab, temperature and salinity loggers running continuously since 2019. But the aquaculture component has been ad hoc. Visiting researchers run short projects, nobody owns the long-term vision. They want someone to impose coherence. The waters around Lofoten are interesting for kelp. The currents are strong, nutrient flux is high, and the temperature regime is moderated by the Gulf Stream branch. From a purely scientific standpoint, it is an excellent site.

Those are strong fundamentals -- continuous environmental data going back six years and good hydrographic conditions. The lack of a coherent long-term programme is actually an opportunity: you'd get to design the research agenda rather than inherit someone else's framework.

The split arrangement is the part that would concern me practically. Two weeks in Lofoten, two weeks in Tromsoe -- that's a lot of context-switching. How would it interact with your existing responsibilities? Do you run a team in Tromsoe that would need you present?

I run a team of six in Tromsoe. That is the primary complication. They are competent, and two of them are senior enough to handle day-to-day decisions in my absence, but two weeks per month is not a small absence. I would need to restructure how we coordinate. Remote supervision is possible but not ideal for lab work.

That's a real constraint. Lab supervision in particular doesn't translate well to remote management -- you can video-call a meeting, but you can't video-call a contaminated culture or a miscalibrated instrument. Your two senior people would essentially be acting as co-leads during your Lofoten weeks.

Have you thought about whether this could be structured differently -- say, one week per month instead of two, or concentrated into seasonal blocks that align with the field season?

I have considered seasonal blocks. The kelp work in Lofoten would be most intensive from March through September, so in principle I could concentrate my presence there during those months and be more consistently in Tromsoe through the winter. That would make the logistics less disruptive. But the station has framed it as a regular biweekly rotation, and I am not certain they would accommodate a different structure.

It might be worth proposing the seasonal block model before assuming they won't consider it. Institutions often frame positions in standard terms because nobody has offered an alternative. If you can make the case that concentrated presence during the growing season produces better science than evenly distributed visits, that's an argument most research directors would listen to.

Is there something beyond the logistics pulling you toward this? You mentioned the site is scientifically excellent, but there are many excellent sites. What makes this one worth the disruption?

The Lofoten kelp habitat is, as far as I can determine, the best site in northern Norway for the next phase of my research. The combination of current velocity, temperature stability, and depth profile is difficult to find elsewhere. We have been running lab simulations and small-scale trials in Tromsoe, but the conditions there are not representative of what a scaled kelp aquaculture system would encounter. Lofoten is. That is the scientific case. There are also personal dimensions, but they are less relevant to the decision.

The scientific case sounds strong on its own -- if Lofoten offers conditions that Tromsoe can't replicate, and your research is at the stage where it needs representative field conditions, then the site matters regardless of any personal dimensions.

That said, personal dimensions are never entirely irrelevant to a career decision that involves restructuring your life every two weeks. You don't have to share them, but it's worth being honest with yourself about whether they're pulling you toward the position or pushing you away from it.

Have you discussed this with your department in Tromsoe? A split appointment would presumably need institutional approval from both sides.

I have not discussed it formally with my department yet. I wanted to think it through before introducing it as a possibility, because once you raise something like this with an institution it acquires momentum that can be difficult to redirect. My father fished those waters. He knew the currents around Lofoten better than any oceanographer I have met, though he would never have described it in those terms. I do not make decisions based on sentiment, but I notice that the scientific rationale and the personal geography happen to converge, and I am trying to determine whether that convergence is clarifying or distorting my judgment.

That's an unusually honest piece of self-examination. The fact that you're explicitly questioning whether the personal connection is biasing your scientific assessment suggests you're already guarding against it. The risk would be greater if you didn't notice the convergence at all.

One way to test it: evaluate the Lofoten site the same way you'd evaluate any field station -- write up the criteria your next research phase requires, score Lofoten against them, and score two or three alternative sites for comparison. If Lofoten still comes out on top when you've formalized the evaluation, then the personal geography isn't distorting anything. It's just coincidence.

And if it doesn't come out on top but you still want to go -- that's useful information too.

That is a reasonable approach. I will construct a site comparison matrix before I raise it with the department. Hydrographic conditions, existing infrastructure, logistic cost, personnel implications, distance from Tromsoe. If Lofoten survives a structured evaluation, I can present it with confidence. If it does not, I will have saved myself a complicated conversation. I have not decided anything. I am only at the stage of determining whether there is a decision worth making.

That's exactly the right framing. Deciding whether to decide is underrated as a step -- most people skip it and end up committed before they've evaluated the options. The site comparison matrix will either give you a clear case to bring to your department or a clear reason to set the idea aside. Either outcome is useful.

When you do build the matrix, weight the criteria before you score the sites. It's easy to unconsciously adjust weights after the fact to favour the answer you already want. Set the weights first, then score.

Noted. Weights before scores. I will work on this over the next week. The polar night is arriving, which means fewer distractions and more time for structured thinking. Thank you for helping me separate the components.
The dark has its uses. Good luck with the matrix. If you want a second pass on the criteria or the weighting scheme once you've drafted it, you know where to find me.
